The verse holds together the introduction. The verse displays the opening of the narrative that lays bare the spiritual disorder of Israel in the days of the judges, when each man followed his own way in religion. The verse displays the truth that the want of a faithful rule and a settled worship left every household to its own devices, breeding idolatry and confusion.

"In those days there was no king in Israel, but every man did that which was right in his own eyes." The verse displays the Levite consecrated, and Micah's false confidence in having a Levite priest (vv.11-13).
11:
"And the Levite was content to dwell with the man; and the young man was unto him as one of his sons." The very brief verse notes the Levite content to dwell with Micah as one of his sons. The grammatical voices note the contentment. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the Levite was content to dwell with the man; and the young man was unto him as one of his sons" — the Levite settled contentedly into Micah's idolatrous service; the and the Levite was content to dwell with the man (the contentment); the and the young man was unto him as one of his sons (treated as a son); the figure (the minister content in the service of false worship for an easy living; the truth that the Levite was content to serve Micah's idolatry for his keep, Philippians 3:19; the figure of the hireling content in a corrupt service, the conscience silenced by ease; the contentment of the Levite; the idolatrous service, vv.10). The verse holds together the contentment. The verse displays the minister settling comfortably into a false worship, his conscience quieted by provision and place. The verse displays the truth that an easy living and a kind treatment can reconcile a man to a service he ought to abhor.

On "And the Levite was content to dwell with the man," the witnesses note the contentment. Gill notes the Levite content in Micah's service (the ease, Philippians 3:19) — the contentment (content to dwell).

On "and the young man was unto him as one of his sons," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the treatment: "the young man was unto him as one of his sons" — the Levite treated as a son (the kindness); the figure (the hireling content in a corrupt service); the providence (the contentment, Micah's false confidence to follow, vv.12-13). The verse displays the Levite content in Micah's service.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the contentment — "the Levite was content to dwell with the man; and the young man was unto him as one of his sons," the contentment (Keil, Gill); "And the Levite was content to dwell with the man," the contentment (Philippians 3:19, Gill); "and the young man was unto him as one of his sons," the treatment (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the contentment. The verse marks the Levite: content. The verse displays the service: idolatrous. The verse displays the treatment: as a son. The verse displays the conscience: silenced. The contentment displays the hireling content in a corrupt service, the conscience silenced by ease. The verse displays the ease: enjoyed. The verse displays the worship: false. The verse displays the minister: settled. The Levite content in Micah's service displays the hireling content in a corrupt service: the Levite was content to dwell with the man; and the young man was unto him as one of his sons (Philippians 3:19) — the Levite settling contentedly into Micah's idolatrous service for his keep, the hireling content in a corrupt service, the conscience silenced by ease. The verse displays Micah consecrating the Levite as his priest (vv.12).
12:
"And Micah consecrated the Levite; and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah." The very brief verse notes Micah consecrating the Levite as his priest. The grammatical voices note the consecration. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Micah consecrated the Levite; and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah" — Micah, without authority, made the Levite his priest; the and Micah consecrated the Levite (the consecration); the and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah (the priest installed); the figure (the unlawful consecration, a man taking on himself to make a priest; the truth that Micah presumed to consecrate a priest, against God's order, Hebrews 5:4; the figure of the usurped authority that makes its own ministers, the priesthood profaned; the consecration of the Levite; the priest installed, vv.5). The verse holds together the consecration.

On "And Micah consecrated the Levite," the witnesses note the consecration. Gill notes Micah consecrating the Levite without authority (the usurpation, Hebrews 5:4) — the consecration (Micah consecrated).

On "and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the installation: "the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah" — the Levite installed as Micah's priest (the priesthood profaned); the figure (the usurped authority that makes its own ministers); the providence (the consecration, Micah's false confidence to follow, vv.13). The verse displays Micah consecrating the Levite as his priest.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the consecration — "Micah consecrated the Levite; and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah," the consecration (Keil, Gill); "And Micah consecrated the Levite," the consecration (Hebrews 5:4, Gill); "and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah," the installation (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the consecration. The verse marks Micah: consecrating. The verse displays the Levite: made priest. The verse displays the authority: usurped. The verse displays the priesthood: profaned. The consecration displays the usurped authority that makes its own ministers, the priesthood profaned. The verse displays the order: broken. The verse displays the priest: installed. The verse displays the worship: false. Micah consecrating the Levite as his priest displays the usurped authority that makes its own ministers: Micah consecrated the Levite; and the young man became his priest, and was in the house of Micah (Hebrews 5:4) — Micah, without authority, presuming to make the Levite his priest, the usurped authority that makes its own ministers, the priesthood profaned. The verse displays Micah's false confidence that the LORD would do him good (vv.13).
13:
"Then said Micah, Now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest." The witnesses note Micah's false confidence, that the LORD would bless him because he had a Levite for his priest — the crux of the false confidence that rests on outward form and observance while the heart and the worship are corrupt. On "Now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest," interpreters expound the delusion. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the false confidence in form — "then said Micah, Now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest": Micah persuades himself of God's favour because he has secured a Levite, though his whole worship is idolatrous; the confidence resting on the office, not on obedience; the man persuading himself of favour while provoking God); the truth that no correctness of form can sanctify a false worship, and that to trust in the outward while disobeying God is vain, "will ye steal, murder... and come and stand before me in this house... and say, We are delivered?" Jeremiah 7:9-10; The figure of the false confidence that rests on outward form and observance while the heart and the worship are corrupt.

On "then said Micah, Now know I that the LORD will do me good," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the self-flattering confidence. Keil and Ellicott note Micah persuading himself of God's favour and blessing in the midst of his idolatry, the conscience quieted by a form (the false confidence, Matthew 15:8) — the delusion (now know I the LORD will do me good).

On "seeing I have a Levite to my priest," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the confidence in form. Calvin and Gill note Micah resting his confidence on having a Levite, a single point of outward conformity, as if it could sanctify an idolatrous worship, the false peace of formal religion that God never owns (the form without obedience, Jeremiah 7:9-10; Luke 18:9) — the figure (the false peace of formal religion, the confidence built on observances God never owns); the providence (the delusion, the Danites coming to seize the idol and the priest to follow, ch.18). The verse displays Micah's false confidence in having a Levite priest.

The witnesses agree the substance: Micah persuades himself of God's favour because he has secured a Levite, though his whole worship is idolatrous (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of false confidence: now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest, the conscience quieted by a form while the substance was abomination, men deceiving themselves by resting on outward forms while the heart is corrupt (Matthew 15:8), no correctness of form sanctifying a false worship (Jeremiah 7:9-10), the false peace of formal religion God never owns (Luke 18:9). The verse displays the false-confidence crux. Micah's false confidence in having a Levite priest displays the crux: Now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest (Matthew 15:8; Jeremiah 7:9-10; Luke 18:9) — the false confidence that rests on outward form and observance while the heart and the worship are corrupt, the conscience quieted by a form while the substance is abomination, God pleased not by the form but by the obedient heart. The verse displays the Danites coming to seize Micah's idol and his priest (ch.18).
